text: The video shows a large uterus, which is visually prominent in the surgical field during the laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins the hysterectomy procedure by cutting the round ligament, a step typically performed at the start of a total laparoscopic hysterectomy.
The surgeon uses an ultrasonic scalpel to efficiently cut through tissue during the laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully identifies the artery located just below the round ligament, then proceeds to treat and safely cut the fallopian tube using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon prepares hemostasis of the uterine ovarian pedicle using bipolar energy for better control, as the harmonic scalpel is insufficient for safe hemostasis in this area. The pedicle is then cut safely.
The surgeon addresses the ligaments surrounding the large uterus, preparing them for dissection.
The surgeon points out the addexal vein located just above the ovary, highlighting its position in the surgical field.
The surgeon coagulates the adnexal vein using electrocautery, facilitating the opening of the broad ligament. The ovary is conserved due to the patient's young age.
The uterine manipulator visibly pushes the uterus upward, while the gas insufflation aids in the dissection of the bladder, creating space between the bladder and the uterus.
The surgeon performs a dry, bloodless dissection, with no visible bleeding in the surgical field.
The assistant uses an instrument to retract the bladder cranially, moving it away from the surgical field.
The surgeon moves from one round ligament to the other, visually navigating across the pelvic region during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon cuts both round ligaments to increase the mobility of the uterus, preparing for the dissection of the bladder.
The surgeon identifies and controls small bleeders in the surgical field to ensure a safe working environment during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon pushes the uterus towards the left side of the patient, enhancing access to the surgical field.
The surgeon performs hemostasis to control small bleeders, ensuring a clear surgical field during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon cuts the connective tissue to enhance the mobility of the uterus, following the previous dissection of the round ligaments.
The surgeon controls small bleeders in the surgical field to ensure safe progress during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the right round ligament, ensuring safe separation of the ligament from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon controls the artery located just below the round ligament, performing small hemostasis procedures to manage bleeding. The fallopian tube is also being controlled during this step.
The surgeon uses an ultrasonic scalpel to control the round ligament and fallopian tube, while the utero-ovarian vessels are controlled with bipolar cautery due to their larger size.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps to safely coagulate the utero-ovarian pedicle, pushing the uterus medially to gain better access and control over the pedicle.
The surgeon identifies a corpus luteum on the right ovary, a physiological structure. The bowel is visible just below, requiring careful avoidance of thermal injury during the procedure.
The surgeon switches to the harmonic scalpel and targets the area of previous bipolar coagulation to control the utero-ovarian pedicle, ensuring precise tissue manipulation.
The surgeon pushes the uterus medially to gain safe access to the utero-ovarian pedicle, preparing for further dissection and control of the pedicle.
The surgeon begins by coagulating the ligament using a surgical device, preparing to cut the ligament once coagulation is complete.

The surgeon identifies the left adnexal vein, noting the presence of a large vein that requires careful control. Using a bipolar device, the surgeon coagulates the vein before proceeding to cut with the harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon performs coagulation to control the right adnexal vein, facilitating the opening of the brow pigments for safe access to the uterosacral ligament.
The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to cut the utero-ovarian pedicle, following previous bipolar coagulation for vessel control.
The surgeon has controlled the left adnexal vein and proceeds to move medially, increasing the mobility of the uterus for further surgical access.
The surgeon opens the posterior part of the broad ligament by cutting the peritoneum, revealing the uterosacral ligament and the uterine structure.
The surgeon identifies the uterosacral ligament and proceeds to remove the overlying peritoneum, exposing the ligament for further dissection.

The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine vessels, identifying the uterine artery and the uterine vein. Dissection is performed between the uterine artery and the peritoneum to increase the mobility of the uterus.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to dissect in the upper part of the left uterosacral ligament, moving posteriorly to increase the mobility of the uterus.
The surgeon performs a vesico-uterine dissection, opening the Halman plane using a harmonic scalpel, which is visible as a precise tool for cutting and coagulating tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the uterine vessel, which is located close to the medial part of the bladder pillar, ensuring not to damage the artery during the cutting process.
The surgeon coagulates vessels within the vesico-vaginal plane to ensure a clear dissection area, preparing for further separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon demonstrates the harmonic scalpel, highlighting its two components: a mobile part and a fixed part.
The surgeon uses the cold part of the harmonic scalpel to perform a safe dissection, avoiding burning the tissue while manipulating the instrument.
The surgeon identifies a small vessel that needs to be controlled before proceeding with the bladder dissection, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses sharp coagulation to increase the dissection plane, carefully manipulating the tissue to avoid damage to nearby structures.
The surgeon exposes the space between the bladder and the vagina, preparing for further dissection in this anatomical region.
The surgeon visually identifies the medial part of the bladder pillar during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon addresses varicosity by performing complete vascular control of small vessels, ensuring they are managed before proceeding with further dissection.
The surgeon visually identifies the medial part of the bladder pilar, preparing for further dissection in the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon cuts the upper part of the bladder pilar, which is the vascular portion, ensuring there is no risk to surrounding structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the uterine artery and uterine vein, ensuring not to damage these structures while avoiding ureteral or nerve injury. The vena is visible just below the dissection area.
The uterine manipulator is positioned in the surgical field, visible within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon prepares to control the uterine vessels, focusing on the uterine artery and vein visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon removes small connective tissue surrounding the uterine pedicle to enhance the effectiveness of coagulation, clearing the area for better visualization.
The surgeon controls the uterine pedicle, likely using surgical instruments to manage the blood supply and stabilize the structure during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon applies energy using forceps until no bubbles are visible between the two arms, indicating effective coagulation of the tissue.
The surgeon observes the absence of bubbles between the forceps, indicating that the tissue is dry and ready to be safely cut without risk of hemorrhage.
The surgeon coordinates the coagulation of the vessel over a span of two to three centimeters, aiming to minimize bleeding from the remaining uterine tissue.

The surgeon controls the vein using electrocautery, indicated by the presence of bubbles, while managing the surrounding tissue. Another artery is visible nearby, and the vein is further controlled using the same technique.
The surgeon controls the left cervical vaginal vessel using a technique that involves securing the pedicle at a distance of two to three centimeters, ensuring the vessel is safely managed.
The surgeon moves to the opposite side of the surgical field to continue controlling the cervical vaginal vessels, using the same technique as previously applied.
The surgeon is on the right side of the uterus, cutting through the tissue to expose the right uterine belly.
The surgeon uses the same technique to remove the connecting tissue, aiming to locate the uterine artery.
The surgeon carefully navigates the right part of the uterus with limited visibility, avoiding damage to a large pedicle while preparing to perform hemostasis on it.

The surgeon carefully cuts a small section of the right uterine pedicle to improve access to the uterine artery. The surrounding peritoneum is being removed to isolate the artery, facilitating better visualization and access.
The surgeon performs short-duration coagulation on the tissue, creating visible bubbles between the arms of the coagulation device.
The surgeon efficiently controls the right uterine artery, with visible blood inside the vessel. The surgical field shows progressive movement up and down the isthmus.
The surgeon lateralizes the uterus to the opposite side of the pedicle, increasing the distance between the uterus and the pedicle for better access and visualization.
The surgeon cuts the uterine artery, which is visually isolated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully cuts the uterine vein located between the two arms of the bipolar forceps, ensuring precision during this part of the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon controls the cervical vaginal pedicle before performing a colpotomy, using a hook to cut and manage the pedicle.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps to control the uterine artery, ensuring hemostasis by applying energy to the vessel.
The surgeon carefully retracts the bladder away from the dissection area to minimize the risk of thermal injury during the procedure.
The surgeon prepares to perform a colpotomy, using a surgical hook to incise the vaginal cuff after controlling the cervical vaginal pedicle.
The surgeon uses a uterine manipulator and a hook to perform a colpotomy, moving the hook from right to left along the uterine manipulator.
The surgeon cuts the posterior part of the vagina laterally, just above both uterosacral ligaments.
The surgeon extracts a large uterus, containing a ten-centimeter myoma, through the vaginal opening.
The surgeon closes the vaginal incision using a Vicryl Zero suture with a fourteen millimeter needle, carefully stitching the tissue to ensure proper closure.
The surgeon begins the procedure on the lateral part of the vagina, preparing for further surgical steps.
The surgeon inserts a needle into the surgical field, preparing for suturing or tissue manipulation.

The surgeon carefully incorporates the mucosa into the knot to prevent post-operative bleeding, using an extracorporeal knotting technique with a rudder knot that visibly locks itself. The peritoneum is also included in the stitch.
The surgeon takes the lateral edge of the vagina on the opposite side, identifying a bleeder and securing it with a stitch.
The surgeon places an interrupted suture, capturing the entire vaginal tissue and incorporating the peritoneum within the stitch using a Vicryl Zero suture and a fourteen millimeter needle.
The surgeon observes that the bleeding has stopped due to the pressure of the stitch. They proceed to close the medial part of the vagina, carefully avoiding inclusion of the bladder in the stitch.
The surgeon carefully positions the needle from one part of the surgical field to another, ensuring the needle's trajectory avoids penetrating the bladder.
The surgeon performs a suture, inserting the needle into the peritoneum, ensuring proper placement within the tissue layers.
The surgeon performs an intracorporeal mounting, continuing the suturing process within the surgical field.
The surgeon ties two flat knots in the same position and one knot on the opposite side, securing the suture in place.
The surgeon ties the final knot to secure the suture, ensuring the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon locates the anterior and posterior parts of the vagina during the hysterectomy procedure, ensuring precise identification of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon places a stitch inside the peritoneum, securing the suture within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon closes the vaginal cuff using wire knotting tape, securing the tissue with visible knots.
The surgeon ties the final knot, securing the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon inspects the closed vaginal cuff, ensuring the sutures are secure and the closure is complete.
The surgeon observes the right ovary, noting its excessive mobility, and performs an ovariopexy to stabilize the ovary and reduce the risk of ovariitis.
The surgeon secures the round ligament, fallopian tube, and ovary, preparing to perform an ovariopexy.
The surgeon secures a knot to lock it in place, ensuring stability in the surgical field.
The surgeon manipulates the pelvis control arm, adjusting its position to stabilize the pelvic structures during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates that the ovary is securely positioned, preventing any torsion, with the surrounding structures visibly stabilized.
The ureter is visually identified as being distant from the current surgical working area, ensuring it remains unaffected by the ongoing procedure.
The ureter is visible in the surgical field, positioned away from the primary working area.
The surgeon demonstrates the rotation of the ureter, highlighting its movement and orientation within the surgical field.
The surgical field is clear of any active bleeding, with no visible bleeders present.
